NCAA D1 PIPELINE
Methodology Stagnation HC Trends Dumbbell Projection Race × Gender B/AA Parity Summary
Sports Analytics Research

NCAA D1 Coaching Pipeline
& Demographic Analysis

14 years of data across Women's Basketball, Softball, Volleyball & Soccer — tracking representation, the gender coaching gap, and the structural barriers that determine who coaches at the highest level. Prepared for coaching staff and front-office decision-makers.

🏀 Women's Basketball 🥎 Softball 🏐 Volleyball ⚽ Soccer 2012 – 2025 NCAA D-I Krystal Beasley · krystalbcreative.com
Methodology Dataset & Approach
In [1]:
Methodology

Dataset & Approach

This analysis draws from the NCAA D-I Coaching Demographics Dataset (2012–2025) — a 14-year longitudinal record covering coaching staff demographics, student-athlete rosters, and role-level representation across Division I women's sports.

  • 17,332 records across Coaching Staff and Student-Athlete groups
  • Focus sports: Women's Basketball, Softball, Volleyball, and Soccer
  • Key metrics: female HC%, racial composition by role, Shannon Diversity Index (SDI), parity projections
  • Projections use ordinary least-squares linear regression (2012–2025) extended forward at constant rate of change
Audience note: This report is designed for coaching staff and front office decision-makers. Each section leads with the finding, followed by the data. Methodology notes appear in code cells.
In [2]:
# Core data pipeline
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import linregress

df  = pd.read_parquet("ncaa_d1_womens_sports.parquet")   # 17,332 rows × 52 cols
cs  = df[df['group_type'] == 'Coaching Staff']
sa  = df[df['group_type'] == 'Student-Athlete']

# Sport color mapping (consistent across all visuals)
COLORS = {"Basketball": "#C88F5A", "Softball": "#4F6D7A",
          "Volleyball": "#7A9B9E", "Soccer":   "#B8956A"}
Finding 1 The Stagnation Story: Female HC% Barely Moved in 14 Years
In [3]:
Finding 1

The Stagnation Story: Female Head Coaching Has Barely Moved in 14 Years

The heatmap below shows female head coach percentage for every D-I sport tracked from 2012 to 2025. The color scale runs from warm copper (lower female HC%) to cool navy (higher female HC%). The nearly uniform horizontal banding — year over year, sport over sport — is the central finding:

65%
WBB female HC% in 2025 — highest of the four focus sports
31%
Soccer female HC% in 2025 — lowest, barely moved since 2012
<5pp
Average annual change across all tracked sports — near-zero movement

The slope panel (right side of the chart) shows the annual rate of change for each sport. Most bars are effectively flat. This is not a pipeline slowly catching up — it is structural inertia.

In [4]:
# Female HC% pivot: sport × year
hc = cs[(cs['gender'] == 'Women') & (cs['role'] == 'Head Coach')]
   .drop_duplicates(['sport', 'year'])

hc['pct'] = hc['gender_pct_within_role'] * 100
pivot = hc.pivot_table(index='sport', columns='year', values='pct')

# Slope per sport: linear regression pp/year
slope = pivot.apply(lambda row: linregress(pivot.columns, row.values).slope, axis=1)
Out [5]:

Heatmap of female head coach % by sport (rows) and year (columns). Right panel shows annual rate of change in percentage points per year. Warm tones = lower female HC%; cool navy = higher female HC%. Horizontal banding indicates near-zero movement across most sports.

Finding 2 Disaggregated HC Trends by Sport (2012–2025)
In [6]:
Finding 2

Disaggregated: Female Head Coach Trends by Sport (2012–2025)

Breaking the data out by sport reveals that the aggregate picture hides divergent trajectories. Women's Basketball and Softball crossed the 50% threshold years ago and have largely plateaued. Volleyball is approaching parity but stalling. Soccer has been flat for over a decade.

+13pp
WBB female HC% gain 2012–2025 — plateau since ~2019
+8pp
Softball — crossed 50% earliest of all focus sports
+9pp
Volleyball — approaching 50%, but rate of change is slowing
+3pp
Soccer — 14 years, effectively flat

The dumbbell chart (Finding 3) is the clearest summary. The trend lines here show the texture beneath that summary — where progress happened and where it stopped.

In [7]:
# Small multiples: female HC% over time per sport
for sport in focus_sports:
    d = hc_pivot[sport].dropna()
    slope, intercept, *_ = linregress(d.index, d.values)
    ax.plot(d.index, d.values, color=COLORS[sport], lw=2.5)
    ax.axhline(50, ls='--', lw=0.8, color='#C5CDD1')  # 50% parity line
Out [8]:

Female head coach % by sport, 2012–2025. Top panels: six-sport small multiples with 50% parity reference line. Bottom panel: dumbbell showing 2012 vs. 2025 positions across all tracked sports.

Finding 3 13 Years Later: Where the Four Focus Sports Land
In [9]:
Finding 3

13 Years Later: Where the Four Focus Sports Actually Land

Each dumbbell shows 2012 (hollow) versus 2025 (filled). The gap between them is the total progress over 14 years. The 50% line divides sports that have achieved representation from those still working toward it.

65.2%
WBB female HC% in 2025 — above parity, plateau since 2019
69.8%
Softball — highest of focus sports; at parity since early 2010s
46.8%
Volleyball — closest to parity but rate of change is slowing
30.8%
Soccer — 35pp behind Basketball, virtually unchanged in a decade
The plateau problem: Even in sports above 50%, progress has stalled. Basketball has been between 60–66% since 2018. Softball between 65–72% since 2016. The question is no longer just "when do we get there?" — it's "why did we stop moving once we got close?"
In [10]:
# Dumbbell chart: 2012 vs. 2025 for 4 focus sports
for i, sport in enumerate(FOCUS_4):
    v12 = pivot.loc[sport, 2012]
    v25 = pivot.loc[sport, 2025]
    color = COLORS[sport.replace("Women's ", "")]
    ax.plot([v12, v25], [i, i], color=color, lw=3.5, zorder=3)
    ax.scatter([v12], [i], facecolors='white', edgecolors=color, s=140)  # 2012
    ax.scatter([v25], [i], color=color, s=180)                               # 2025
Out [11]:

Dumbbell: female head coach % in 2012 (hollow) vs. 2025 (filled) for the four focus sports. Vertical dashed line at 50% marks gender parity. Basketball and Softball are above parity; Volleyball approaches it; Soccer lags by 35 percentage points.

Finding 4 Projection: When Does 50% Parity Arrive?
In [12]:
Finding 4

Projection: At Current Rates, When Does 50% Parity Arrive?

Using the 2012–2025 linear trend for each sport, this analysis projects how many years it takes to reach 50% female head coaches at the current rate of change. The answer is stark.

Already
Basketball & Softball — above 50% parity since the early data period
2035
Volleyball projected 50% parity year — ~10 years if current trend holds
2108
Soccer projected 50% parity year — 83+ years from now at today's pace
What this tells coaching staff and front office: If your sport is not currently above parity, the pipeline will not fix itself. Volleyball's 2035 window is achievable with intentional hiring and development. Soccer's 2108 projection is a policy failure in plain numbers.
In [13]:
# Parity projection: solve for reach_year at constant slope
for sport, data in projections.items():
    slope, current = data['slope'], data['current_hc']

    if slope > 0.05 and current < 50:
        reach_year = int(2025 + (50 - current) / slope)
    elif current >= 50:
        reach_year = "Already achieved"
    else:
        reach_year = "Trend stalled"

# Assumes linear (constant) rate — actual may be faster or slower
Out [14]:

Left panel: female HC% trend lines (solid, historical) with projected extensions (dashed). Diamond = projected year of 50% parity. Right panel: horizontal bars showing years until parity from 2025. Basketball and Softball marked 'already achieved.' Soccer projects to 2108.

Finding 5 The Double Glass Ceiling: B/AA Women in Head Coaching
In [15]:
Race & Gender Intersection

The Double Glass Ceiling: Black/AA Women in Head Coaching

When you filter the female head coaching story to Black and African American women specifically, a sharper pattern emerges. Even in sports where women have made gains, B/AA women are underrepresented relative to their share of the athlete pipeline — facing both a gender barrier and a racial one simultaneously.

−10.5pp
WBB: B/AA athlete share (46%) vs. B/AA female HC share (35.5%) — 2025
−2.8pp
Volleyball: B/AA female HC% actually declined since 2012 — moving away from parity
+13.5pp
WBB B/AA female HC% growth 2012–2025 — the one positive exception
Each panel shows three lines: overall female HC% (dashed), B/AA women as % of female HCs (solid), and B/AA athlete % (dotted reference target). The shaded gap between solid and dotted lines is the coaching pipeline deficit for B/AA women specifically.
In [16]:
# Two-step merge to compute B/AA female HC%
# Step 1: total women HCs per sport/year
women_hc['women_total'] = (
    women_hc['gender_pct_within_role'] * women_hc['total_head_coaches']
).round(0)

# Step 2: B/AA women HC count
baa_hc = cs[(cs['gender'] == 'Women') &
            (cs['role'] == 'Head Coach') &
            (cs['race_ethnicity_normalized'] == 'Black or African American')]

# B/AA women as % of female HCs (not all HCs)
merged['baa_pct'] = merged['baa_count'] / merged['women_total'] * 100
Out [17]:

2×2 small multiples for Basketball, Softball, Volleyball, and Soccer. Each panel: overall female HC% (dashed), B/AA women as % of female HCs (solid), B/AA athlete % (dotted). Shaded region = coaching-to-athlete representation gap for B/AA women specifically.

Finding 6 Racing to Representation: B/AA Female HC vs. Athlete Share
In [18]:
Race-Specific Projection

Racing to Representation: When Do B/AA Female HCs Match Their Athletes?

This final analysis reframes the parity question — instead of asking "when does female HC% reach 50%?" it asks: when does B/AA female HC% reach the share that B/AA athletes already represent in each sport? — a more meaningful benchmark using the actual pipeline as the target.

~2035
WBB: projected year B/AA female HC% reaches B/AA athlete share (~46%)
Already
Softball: B/AA female HC% (~8.5%) at B/AA athlete share (~8.4%) — only sport at race parity
Stalled
Volleyball: B/AA female HC% declining — moving away from athlete rep parity
For talent development staff: Softball's B/AA parity success is not an accident — it reflects structural conditions where the player-to-coach path has historically been more accessible. Understanding what drives that outcome in one sport is the most actionable question this data raises.
In [19]:
# B/AA parity projection: reach_year when baa_hc% ≥ ath_target%
for sport, info in projections.items():
    current_hc  = info['current_hc']      # B/AA female HC% in 2025
    target      = info['target_current']  # B/AA athlete% in 2025
    slope       = info['slope']

    if slope > 0.05 and target > current_hc:
        reach_year = int(2025 + (target - current_hc) / slope)
    elif current_hc >= target:
        reach_year = "At parity ✓"
    else:
        reach_year = "Trend stalled"
Out [20]:

Top 2×2: B/AA female HC% (solid) with B/AA athlete% reference line (dotted) and shaded gap per sport. Diamond markers = projected year of parity. Bottom row: summary mini-bars comparing current B/AA female HC% to athlete representation target, with parity status badges.

Summary What the Data Demands
In [21]:
Summary

What the Data Demands

Five visuals. Six interconnected findings. One throughline: the NCAA D-I coaching workforce is not self-correcting toward representation. The stagnation is structural, the timelines are long, and the barriers compound for Black and African American women in particular.

For Coaching Staff

  • Sports above 50% female HCs (Basketball, Softball) have plateaued — success is not continuing to grow on its own momentum.
  • Volleyball has a realistic window to reach parity (~2035) with intentional hiring practices.
  • Soccer requires structural intervention. At current rates, parity is a century away.

For Front Office & Athletic Directors

  • The "wait for the pipeline" argument is not supported by this data. Athletes are diversifying significantly faster than coaching ranks.
  • Softball demonstrates race-specific coaching parity is achievable. Understanding what drives that outcome in one sport is the most actionable question in this dataset.
  • Black/AA women face compounding barriers: underrepresented as female coaches, and further underrepresented relative to their share of the athlete pipeline among female coaches. Both must be addressed, not just one.
"The data exists. Now comes the work."
This analysis identifies where structural barriers exist and quantifies their scale. The decisions about what to do with that knowledge belong to coaching staff, front office, and athletic leadership — the people in a position to act on it.
Krystal Beasley · Sports Analytics Portfolio · krystalbcreative.com
Source: NCAA D-I Coaching Demographics Dataset, 2012–2025, published September 2025 on NCAA.org.
All analysis, visualizations, and projections: Krystal Beasley. Linear projections assume constant rate of change.
